assumptions: The question relates to consolidated financial statements.
The intragroup dividend has already been recognised as a receivable/payable, so the exposure is analysed as an existing balance rather than a forecast transaction.
A foreign currency exposure exists only if the intragroup monetary balance is between entities with different functional currencies.

approaches.label: Foreign currency hedge of a recognized intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 provides a specific exception allowing foreign currency risk on an intragroup monetary item, such as a receivable/payable, to qualify as a hedged item in consolidated financial statements if the FX effects are not fully eliminated on consolidation.
conditions: the balance is a monetary item
the exposure is in consolidated financial statements
the entities have different functional currencies
the FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
the hedge is formally designated and documented at inception
the hedge effectiveness requirements in IFRS 9 paragraph 6.4.1 are met
approaches.practical_implication: The FX risk on the recognised intragroup dividend receivable can be documented in a hedge relationship only if this intragroup monetary-item exception is satisfied.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows foreign currency risk on an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements when the FX effect is not fully eliminated.
Paragraph 6.4.1 requires formal designation, documentation and effectiveness assessment at inception.

approaches.label: General prohibition on intragroup hedged items in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: The default IFRS 9 rule is that only exposures with parties external to the reporting entity can be designated as hedged items in consolidated financial statements; intragroup items are excluded unless a specific exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: If the dividend-related FX exposure does not meet a specific intragroup exception, hedge accounting is not available in consolidation.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 restricts hedged items in consolidation to external-party exposures, subject to limited exceptions.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of a highly probable forecast intragroup transaction
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 separately permits a highly probable forecast intragroup transaction to be hedged for foreign currency risk if it will affect consolidated profit or loss, but that route is for forecast transactions rather than a receivable already recognised.
conditions: the transaction is still forecast and highly probable
it is denominated in a currency other than the functional currency of the entity entering into it
the foreign currency risk will affect consolidated profit or loss
approaches.practical_implication: This approach is relevant before recognition of the dividend, not once the intragroup dividend has become an existing receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits certain highly probable forecast intragroup transactions to qualify as hedged items in consolidated financial statements.
Paragraphs B6.3.5-B6.3.6 explain that the foreign currency risk must affect consolidated profit or loss and that OCI recycling follows when the transaction affects profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 is confined to hedges of net investments in foreign operations and states that it should not be applied by analogy to other hedge accounting situations. A recognised intragroup dividend receivable is not identified in the provided facts as the net investment itself.
approaches.practical_implication: The dividend receivable's FX risk should not be documented as a net investment hedge unless the exposure is genuinely the qualifying net investment in the foreign operation.

source_contributions.summary: The Interpretation provides a separate hedge accounting model for foreign currency risk on net investments in foreign operations.
Paragraph 8 says the Interpretation applies only to net investment hedges and should not be applied by analogy to other hedge accounting types.
